title: 16:10 Aspect Ratio Calculator - Screen & Display Dimensions
tagline: Calculate 16:10 screen dimensions, diagonal size, pixel density, and area for monitors, laptops, and tablets.
intro: Enter width, height, and optionally a diagonal size to calculate all 16:10 display properties including pixel density and area.

error.invalidInput: Please enter valid positive numbers for width and height.

about.heading: About the 16:10 Aspect Ratio Calculator
about.body:
The 16:10 aspect ratio describes a rectangle whose width is 1.6 times its height. Expressed as a ratio, that is 16 parts wide for every 10 parts tall, or equivalently 8:5 in lowest terms. This ratio sits between the older 4:3 standard and the now-dominant 16:9 widescreen format, giving it a slightly taller, more document-like proportions than a modern television.

The 16:10 ratio became particularly popular in laptop displays and professional monitors during the mid-2000s and early 2010s. Apple's MacBook Pro lineup used 16:10 displays for many years, and the ratio returned with Apple Silicon MacBooks. The extra vertical space compared to 16:9 makes 16:10 well-suited to productivity work — spreadsheets, code editors, and document editing all benefit from the additional rows of content visible without scrolling. In photography and graphic design, the ratio is also closer to the proportions of standard paper sizes.

Common 16:10 resolutions include 1280×800 (WXGA), 1440×900 (WXGA+), 1680×1050 (WSXGA+), 1920×1200 (WUXGA), 2560×1600 (WQXGA), and 3840×2400 (UHD+). All of these maintain the exact 16:10 ratio when you divide width by height: 1920÷1200 = 1.6, 2560÷1600 = 1.6, and so on.

This calculator lets you input width and height in pixels, centimetres, inches, or millimetres. It checks whether the entered dimensions conform exactly to the 16:10 ratio, computes the diagonal via the Pythagorean theorem (d = √(w² + h²)), and — when dimensions are in pixels and a physical diagonal is provided — calculates the pixel density in pixels per inch (PPI). The area is simply width × height, useful for comparing screen sizes or estimating the material needed for a screen protector.

Pixel density (PPI) is particularly important for screen sharpness. A 1920×1200 display on a 24-inch screen has a very different visual experience from the same resolution on a 13-inch laptop. Higher PPI values mean text and images appear crisper. Modern "Retina" displays typically exceed 200 PPI. The formula is PPI = diagonal_pixels / diagonal_inches, where diagonal_pixels = √(width² + height²).

Whether you are specifying a custom display, comparing monitor options, designing a user interface at correct proportions, or verifying that a video crop maintains the 16:10 ratio, this calculator provides all the key measurements in one place.

examples.heading: 16:10 Aspect Ratio Examples
examples.intro: Common 16:10 displays from monitors, laptops, and tablets with their dimensions and pixel densities.

items.note: WUXGA resolution at 94 PPI. Standard professional widescreen monitor.
WQXGA resolution at 188 PPI. High-DPI laptop panel.
WXGA resolution at 149 PPI. Compact productivity tablet.
WQXGA at 111 PPI. Premium widescreen desktop monitor.

howto.heading: How to Use the 16:10 Aspect Ratio Calculator
steps: Select the unit for your measurements — pixels, centimetres, inches, or millimetres.
Enter the width of the display in the Width field.
Enter the height of the display in the Height field.
Optionally enter the physical diagonal size (in the same unit) to calculate pixel density (PPI) when using pixels.
Click Calculate to see the aspect ratio check, diagonal, area, and pixel density. Click Reset to clear all fields.

items.a: The 16:10 aspect ratio means the display is 16 units wide for every 10 units tall, equivalent to 8:5 in lowest terms. Dividing width by height always gives exactly 1.6. It is a moderately wide format used mainly in laptops and professional monitors.

A 16:9 display is slightly wider relative to its height — the ratio is approximately 1.778, versus 1.6 for 16:10. This means 16:10 gives about 11% more vertical screen space for the same width, which many users find better for productivity tasks like reading documents and writing code.
Common 16:10 pixel resolutions include 1280×800 (WXGA), 1440×900 (WXGA+), 1680×1050 (WSXGA+), 1920×1200 (WUXGA), 2560×1600 (WQXGA), and 3840×2400 (UHD+). All of these divide to exactly 1.6.

items.q: How do I calculate the diagonal from width and height?
items.a: Use the Pythagorean theorem: diagonal = √(width² + height²). For a 1920×1200 display: √(1920² + 1200²) = √(3686400 + 1440000) = √5126400 ≈ 2264 pixels. Divide by the PPI to get the physical diagonal in inches.

items.q: How do I calculate pixels per inch (PPI)?
items.a: First compute the diagonal in pixels using √(width² + height²), then divide by the physical diagonal in inches. For a 1920×1200 display on a 24-inch screen: PPI = 2264 / 24 ≈ 94. Higher PPI means a sharper-looking display.

Yes — after a decade of 16:9 dominance, 16:10 and taller ratios (3:2, 16:10) are increasingly popular in premium laptops. Apple's MacBook Pro returned to 16:10 in 2021, and many Windows laptop makers followed. The extra height is valued for productivity and professional creative work.
